hook: You already know how to apply the Gram-Schmidt process to turn a basis into an orthonormal set, and you know how to multiply matrices. But here is something you may not have noticed: every time you run Gram-Schmidt on the columns of a matrix $A$, you are secretly building a factorization of $A$ itself. Can you see how those orthonormal vectors and the projection coefficients you computed along the way combine to write $A = QR$, and why anyone would want to do that?
concept: QR decomposition is a way of factoring any matrix with linearly independent columns into two pieces: an orthogonal matrix and an upper triangular matrix. Formally, given an $m \times n$ matrix $A$ with linearly independent columns ($m \geq n$), there exist a matrix $Q$ whose columns are orthonormal and an upper triangular matrix $R$ with positive diagonal entries such that $A = QR$. The factorization is unique under those sign conventions and is produced directly by the Gram-Schmidt process.
anatomy: The decomposition $A = QR$ has three named components. First, the original matrix $A$ is the $m \times n$ matrix whose columns $\mathbf{a}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{a}_n$ form a linearly independent set. Second, $Q$ is the $m \times n$ matrix whose columns $\mathbf{q}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{q}_n$ are the orthonormal vectors produced by Gram-Schmidt applied to the columns of $A$; it satisfies $Q^T Q = I_n$. Third, $R$ is an $n \times n$ upper triangular matrix whose entry $r_{ij}$ in row $i$, column $j$ records the inner product $\langle \mathbf{a}_j, \mathbf{q}_i \rangle$ (the projection coefficient), which is zero whenever $i > j$ precisely because $\mathbf{q}_i$ is orthogonal to all subsequent $\mathbf{a}_j$.

worked_example.description_latex: We start with the matrix $A$ whose two columns we will call $\mathbf{a}_1 = (1,1,0)^T$ and $\mathbf{a}_2 = (1,0,1)^T$. Our goal is to write $A = QR$ by applying Gram-Schmidt to these columns and carefully recording every coefficient we compute along the way.

The first step of Gram-Schmidt simply normalizes $\mathbf{a}_1$, giving the first orthonormal vector $\mathbf{q}_1$. The coefficient $r_{11}$ is the norm of $\mathbf{a}_1$ because when we write $\mathbf{a}_1 = r_{11}\mathbf{q}_1$, the diagonal entry of $R$ captures exactly how long $\mathbf{a}_1$ is in the direction it already points.

The off-diagonal entry $r_{12}$ is the projection of $\mathbf{a}_2$ onto $\mathbf{q}_1$, and we subtract this component from $\mathbf{a}_2$ to get a vector orthogonal to $\mathbf{q}_1$. This subtraction is the heart of Gram-Schmidt, and notice that $r_{12}$ lands in row 1, column 2 of $R$ — above the diagonal — which is exactly where upper triangular matrices have their non-zero off-diagonal entries.

We normalize the residual vector to get $\mathbf{q}_2$, and the norm becomes the second diagonal entry $r_{22}$ of $R$. You can verify that $\langle \mathbf{q}_1, \mathbf{q}_2 \rangle = 0$, confirming the columns of $Q$ are orthonormal.

We assemble $Q$ from the orthonormal columns and $R$ from the coefficients we recorded: the diagonal holds the norms $r_{11}, r_{22}$ and the upper entry holds the projection coefficient $r_{12}$. You should multiply $QR$ explicitly to verify you recover $A$ — this check is always worth doing and it builds intuition for why the triangular structure of $R$ works out perfectly.

partial_example.description_latex: We want the QR decomposition of this matrix. Notice that $\mathbf{a}_1 = (1,0,0)^T$ is already a unit vector, so the first step of Gram-Schmidt is trivial: we set $\mathbf{q}_1 = \mathbf{a}_1$ and record $r_{11} = \|\mathbf{a}_1\| = 1$.
We project $\mathbf{a}_2 = (2,1,0)^T$ onto $\mathbf{q}_1$ to get the off-diagonal coefficient $r_{12} = 2$, and subtracting gives the orthogonal residual $(0,1,0)^T$, which is already a unit vector.

Now assemble the full factorization: write down the matrix $Q$ using the two orthonormal columns you have found, and write down $R$ using the four coefficients $r_{11}, r_{12}, r_{21}, r_{22}$ you have computed (remember $r_{21} = 0$ because $R$ is upper triangular). What are $Q$ and $R$?

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Find the QR decomposition of $A = \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix}$ (a single-column matrix).
Apply Gram-Schmidt to find the QR decomposition of $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.

Find the QR decomposition of $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ and use it to solve $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ with $\mathbf{b} = (2, 2, 2)^T$ by back-substitution on $R\mathbf{x} = Q^T\mathbf{b}$.

A data scientist represents three document vectors as columns of $A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$. Compute $Q$ and $R$ so that $A = QR$, and interpret what the columns of $Q$ represent geometrically in $\mathbb{R}^2$.
practice_problems.answer_latex: Q = \begin{bmatrix} 2/\sqrt{5} & -1/\sqrt{5} \\ 1/\sqrt{5} & 2/\sqrt{5} \end{bmatrix},\quad R = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{5} & 4/\sqrt{5} \\ 0 & 3/\sqrt{5} \end{bmatrix};\text{ columns of }Q\text{ form an orthonormal basis for }\mathbb{R}^2

common_mistakes: Students often record $r_{ij} = \langle \mathbf{a}_j, \mathbf{q}_i \rangle$ correctly but then place it in position $(j, i)$ instead of $(i, j)$ when assembling $R$, producing a lower triangular matrix instead of an upper triangular one. Always remember that the column index of $R$ matches the column of $A$ being processed, so $r_{ij}$ sits in row $i$, column $j$ with $i \leq j$.

After subtracting projection components to form the residual, students frequently forget to normalize the residual before using it as the next column of $Q$, which means $Q^T Q \neq I$ and the factorization $A = QR$ fails. The diagonal entry $r_{kk}$ is precisely the norm of the residual, so if you skip normalization you are also losing the correct value for $r_{kk}$.

When solving $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ via QR, students sometimes compute $Q^T\mathbf{b}$ but then try to invert $Q$ again instead of proceeding directly to back-substitution on $R\mathbf{x} = Q^T\mathbf{b}$. Since $Q^TQ = I$, multiplying both sides by $Q^T$ already isolates $R\mathbf{x}$, and the only remaining work is triangular back-substitution.
untested_variants: QR decomposition of a matrix with more columns than rows (the wide/fat matrix case where full column rank fails), which requires a different treatment or a rank-revealing variant.
Using the Householder reflection or Givens rotation algorithms to compute QR, as opposed to Gram-Schmidt — these are numerically stable alternatives tested in numerical linear algebra courses.

The full (square) QR decomposition where $Q$ is extended to a full $m \times m$ orthogonal matrix by appending $m - n$ additional orthonormal columns, as opposed to the thin/reduced QR used in this lesson.
Applying QR decomposition to compute eigenvalues via the QR algorithm (iterating $A_{k+1} = R_k Q_k$), which combines QR factorization with spectral theory in a way not addressed here.
